data.title: ONE Championship: Rodtang Jitmuangnon vows to ‘fight like a rocket’ against Nong-O Hama
data.description:
Rodtang Jitmuangnon is a pure showman. From going viral for dropping his hands to marching forward with relentless pressure and fire in his gloves, he is the ultimate fan-friendly fighter. There is no “off” switch and there are no backwards steps, even when it is a friend standing across from him in the ring.
Ahead of his flyweight Muay Thai showdown with Nong-O Hama at ONE 173 in Tokyo on Sunday, the Thai superstar said he had no intention of changing his approach.
“This is my style and I cannot change who I am,” Rodtang told the Post in Tokyo. “I love to fight this way. I love to show my personality in the ring. When the opportunity arrives, I have to take it. I will do my best on Sunday.”
It is a match built on respect and history. Nong-O, now 39, is one of the most accomplished Thai fighters of his generation, while Rodtang remains the sport’s most recognisable name.
Both men have worn ONE Championship gold and inspired a new wave of athletes. For Rodtang, this rematch represents more than a belt; it is a chance to reassert himself on the world stage.

“When there is an opportunity, I must grab it,” he said. “It’s time to take my belt back.”
Despite the friendship between them, Rodtang laughed off any suggestion that he might hold back his trademark antics. “Absolutely, I’ll still be myself,” he said with a grin. “Maybe I’ll drop my hands, maybe even climb on his neck. That’s who I am.”
Asked about Nong-O’s age, Rodtang refused to take the bait. “Age is just a number,” he said.
“It’s about who has the better plan and who is better prepared. Nong-O is disciplined, he trains hard and I cannot underestimate him.”


What he believes will separate them is speed. “I will fight like a rocket,” he said. “That’s my advantage. I’ll be faster, and I’ll use that.”
Both fighters are chasing not only victory but also the performance bonuses that have become a hallmark of ONE events. “We will do our best,” Rodtang said. “Everyone wants the belt and the bonus, but in the end it’s up to Chatri, the big boss, to decide.”
Rodtang also addressed some familiar names. Japanese kickboxing star Takeru Segawa, who fought him in one of the biggest combat sports events ever held in Japan, has publicly expressed interest in a rematch.
Rodtang’s response was simple: “Just get that win. If you win, I’m ready to fight you anytime, even bare hand.”
As for Tenshin Nasukawa, who beat Rodtang in an exhibition bout in 2018 before switching to professional boxing, the Thai said there was no bad blood. “Tenshin has his goals in boxing,” Rodtang said. “If he succeeds and wants to come back to kickboxing one day, I’ll be ready to face him again.”
Beyond Tokyo, Rodtang is also excited about the rumoured return of ONE Championship to Kuala Lumpur. His appearance at fight week in traditional Malay attire earned him plenty of attention from Malaysian fans.
“I was happy to wear the Baju Melayu,” he said. “As a Muslim, I’d love to fight in Kuala Lumpur. There are many Muslim fans there, and I want to show my skills for them.”












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by Andrew Whitelaw (@whitelawsport)



Asked who he would want to face if that Malaysia event happens, he smiled. “Anyone,” he said. “Maybe Superlek again. I can fight anyone, anytime.”
Rodtang also shared his thoughts on Superlek’s coming fight with Yuki Yoza on the same Tokyo card. “It’s a hard fight to call,” he said. “Yuki beat Petchtanong Petchfergus and is dangerous with his boxing. I just hope Superlek doesn’t underestimate him. Don’t worry too much about the leg kicks because Yuki likes to fake and throw punches after. I’m supporting Superlek and hope he wins.”
As ever, Rodtang signed off with gratitude. “To all my fans in Thailand and around the world, please send your support,” he said. “All the Thai fighters will give everything at ONE 173. I hope everyone enjoys the show.”

data.title: Hong Kong police veteran jailed 9 weeks for sexually assaulting female officer
data.description:
A veteran Hong Kong police officer has been jailed for nine weeks and fined HK$6,000 (US$772) for twice assaulting a female junior colleague at work earlier this year.
Kwun Tong Court on Friday convicted Senior Inspector Leung Yun-sing, 57, of indecently assaulting the policewoman by embracing her and patting her buttocks at Hung Hom Police Station on February 18, when he appeared to express concern about her job performance.
The inspector, who has 37 years of service, was also found guilty of common assault for grabbing the complainant’s arms under the pretence of giving her work-related advice earlier the same day.
Magistrate Eric Yao Kwok-sun acknowledged that the accused must have a good track record and personal background, as reflected by his senior rank in the force, but said immediate imprisonment was clearly necessary given the seriousness of the sexual assault.
Yao criticised Leung for abusing his seniority to flout the law, but agreed to partially reduce his sentence as the ruling would effectively ruin his future.
The magistrate deducted one-tenth from the starting point of 10 weeks and imposed an additional fine for grabbing the policewoman’s hands.
Leung obtained the court’s permission to defer the custodial term pending the outcome of an appeal at the High Court.
The trial heard that Leung, who headed the police station’s miscellaneous inquiries subunit before his suspension, had held several discussions with the complainant about work arrangements on the day of the offences.
The policewoman said that she went to the inspector’s office at around 3.30pm that day to seek his instructions on whether to recommend a criminal investigation into a security guard’s complaint.
She said Leung grabbed her arms for one to two minutes while purporting to draw an analogy between different classifications of cases.
According to the complainant, the inspector later held her in his arms and patted her buttocks two to three times that evening, asking why she had been so “clumsy” at work recently. She filed a police report after consulting her boyfriend and family members.
Leung denied any physical contact with the junior colleague. His younger sister testified to his integrity and respect for women.
In his verdict, Yao rejected Leung’s inconsistent explanations for holding the policewoman’s arms, finding that the complainant had no motive to frame him, as the pair had previously maintained a good working relationship.
Defence counsel Jacky Lai Tsz-kit pleaded for leniency and said that Leung would likely lose his retirement benefits, including a HK$5 million lump sum gratuity and a monthly pension of HK$30,000.

data.title: From smartphones to smart glasses: Rokid’s latest AI frames join China’s new device race
data.description:
Hangzhou-based AI and augmented-reality (AR) glasses maker Rokid on Thursday unveiled a lightweight, AI-powered eyewear device built in partnership with Chinese eyewear brand Bolon, as rivals race to make smart glasses the next mass-market device after the smartphone.
The new model features a 38.5g D-shaped black frame and supports customised prescription lenses, allowing it to be worn like a regular pair of sunglasses with a more fashion-focused look.
It includes a physical control button and a touchpad on the right temple. Powered by Qualcomm’s AR1 chipset, the glasses can record video, take photos, play music and provide navigation.
“AI glasses are approaching their ‘iPhone moment’,” said Misa Zhu Mingming, Rokid’s CEO and founder.
Despite lacking a display, the new device is integrated with leading Chinese AI models – including Alibaba’s Qwen, DeepSeek, ByteDance’s Doubao and Zhipu – enabling a voice-activated digital assistant.
The frame starts at 2,199 yuan (US$310) and prescription lenses cost extra. It carries the branding of Bolon, a domestic eyewear label now part of the EssilorLuxottica group.
At the launch event in Hangzhou, Rokid also showcased upgrades to the AI glasses it introduced last November, which feature a built-in monochrome green display for functions like a teleprompter.

The company also previewed upcoming features, including playing songs via voice command enabled by Tencent’s QQ Music, shopping for items on JD.com by simply looking at them, and using Alipay to purchase Luckin Coffee beverages.
Start-ups, Zhu said in an interview after the event, were “more flexible, more neutral and more open” in building ecosystem partnerships compared with China’s big tech firms, which he argued were unlikely to commit all their resources to glasses.
Zhu said Rokid would soon launch an app store and pour more resources into its developer ecosystem, pledging 10 million yuan in prize money in next year’s developer competition. He said the company also planned to encourage third parties to design accessories for its glasses.
He added that the orders of Rokid’s AI glasses had exceeded 300,000 units and were “beyond expectations”, which had led to supply-chain bottlenecks earlier this year. He forecast that combined sales across the company’s AI glasses, the new Bolon-branded model and its AR product line, would reach 1 million units next year.
The smart-glasses sector is led globally by Facebook owner Meta, which in September released its latest Ray-Ban-partnered model featuring a full-colour display in the right lens.
By comparison, the device weighs 69g and starts at US$799.
Meanwhile, a wave of Chinese contenders is now emerging. Alibaba Group Holding – which owns the Post – is set to release its Quark AI Glasses on November 27, after opening presales in late October.
Baidu’s Xiaodu AI Glass Pro, priced at 2,299 yuan, went on sale this week. Xiaomi launched its own model in June at 1,999 yuan, while other players include Meizu and RayNeo.
Global shipments of smart glasses surged 110 per cent year-on-year in the first half of the year, driven largely by strong demand for Meta’s devices and new launches such as Xiaomi’s, according to data from research firm Counterpoint.
Shipments of AI glasses were expected to jump 158 per cent in 2025, reaching 5.1 million units, according to research firm Omdia. The market was forecast to surpass 10 million units by 2026 and 35 million units by 2030, it added.

data.title: Bus queue brawl at Malaysia-Singapore crossing puts spotlight on border bottlenecks
data.description:
Viral footage of two commuters wrestling on the floor of Johor Bahru’s main border terminal has once again highlighted the chronic congestion – and fraying tempers – at one of Southeast Asia’s busiest land crossings, where hundreds of thousands travel daily between Malaysia and Singapore.
The 14-second clip, posted late on Wednesday, shows a man and a second passenger who appears to be a woman pushing each other near the fenced entrance to the bus bays inside the Customs, Immigration and Quarantine (CIQ) Complex at the Sultan Iskandar Building.
During the scuffle, they tumble onto the concourse floor as other travellers waiting to board look on in shock.
The video, shared at about 11.50pm in a Malaysia–Singapore commuter group, carried a Chinese caption reading: “Fighting while scrambling to get on the bus.”


Dozens of passengers crowded around the pair as they traded shoves in a narrow queue lane commonly used for late-night departures.
Johor Bahru South District Police Chief Raub Selamat said officers had been given the footage but confirmed that no reports had been lodged.
“The police are identifying the individuals involved, witnesses of the incident and the actual cause of the altercation,” he said in a statement, urging the public not to circulate the clip further to avoid speculation that “could affect public harmony”.
The CIQ complex at BSI is a critical gateway for the near-constant flow of workers, students and shoppers who travel between Malaysia and Singapore.
Studies estimate that about 500,000 Malaysians work in the neighbouring city state, many commuting daily from Johor, and official data show that the two border points – BSI and the Second Link at Tuas – now handle more than 430,000 travellers a day during peak periods.
Johor’s Menteri Besar Onn Hafiz Ghazi has previously said immigration and road-clearance facilities on the Johor side must reach “100 per cent efficiency” to ease congestion.

He said last year that upgrades had shortened Causeway queues by about two hours, though more improvements were still required.
It is not the first fight to be captured at the checkpoint this year.
In April, local media reported that two men were filmed trading punches near the same bus boarding area after one man allegedly tried to cut the bus queue. Both individuals have been summoned by police to assist investigations.
In September, an e-hailing driver and a bus driver were recorded in a separate altercation outside the CIQ entrance. Police later said the dispute began over a parking issue.

data.title: China’s Yangtze River Delta reins in ‘profit-driven’ fines in relief for private sector
data.description:
Entrepreneurs and legal experts have welcomed a move by Shanghai and its neighbouring provinces of Jiangsu, Zhejiang and Anhui to regulate cross-regional market enforcement, saying it will offer relief from arbitrary inspections, overlapping investigations and profit-driven law enforcement.
The four jurisdictions have introduced China’s first mechanism designed to rein in cross-jurisdictional and profit-driven law enforcement, establishing a formal coordination framework to standardise cross-regional enforcement practices.
“If these new measures are effectively implemented and promoted to more provinces across China, they’ll offer much-needed safety and confidence, especially for private enterprises,” said Chris Liang, co-founder of a private technology company based in Guangdong province.
He said there had been many “truly alarming” cases in recent years of private business owners being detained by enforcement authorities from outside their regions who were acting on unclear jurisdictional grounds.
Deng Jianping, a senior lawyer at Guangdong Lawsons Law Office who serves as legal counsel for multiple companies, said many enterprises had reported frequent but unreasonable checks by different departments under various pretexts, which had severely disrupted production and exhausted management resources.
“Amid growing economic headwinds, frequent and disorderly enforcement actions undoubtedly undermine corporate vitality and disrupt normal economic activity,” Deng said.
She urged more provinces to follow the Yangtze River Delta’s move by setting clear boundaries for cross-regional enforcement, which has seen officials travel beyond their jurisdictions to investigate, detain people and levy fines. The practice has become so widespread that it has been likened to fishing boats trawling in offshore waters.
Fiscal stress at the local level is often cited as a major driver of cross-regional enforcement. In the first half of this year, national tax revenue declined by 0.3 per cent year on year, while non-tax revenue – mainly fines – increased by 2 per cent, reaching 2.49 trillion yuan (US$350.5 billion).
China’s non-tax revenue reached 4.47 trillion yuan last year, a year-on-year increase of 25.4 per cent, while tax revenue declined by 3.4 per cent to 17.5 trillion yuan.


Provinces with vibrant private economies, such as Zhejiang, Jiangsu and Guangdong, have often found their entrepreneurs targeted by out-of-town enforcement teams.
Earlier this year, Zhejiang Daily reported that law enforcement officials from outside Zhejiang province had sought to seize assets from private enterprises in cities including Ningbo and Wenzhou. Zhejiang officials intervened to recover the frozen assets and protect the lawful interests of local business owners.
A document outlining the joint mechanism that was released by the four jurisdictions said the new framework clarified the definition and procedural limits for cross-regional enforcement.
If such enforcement was deemed necessary, regulators from other jurisdictions would first have to seek help from their local counterparts and notify local authorities, it said.
At least two enforcement officers from the local jurisdiction would have to be present during any on-site evidence collection.
The mechanism also mandates the mutual recognition of lawfully obtained evidence by the four regions and stipulates that enforcement actions should be suspended when there are jurisdictional disputes.
Xie Zhenghao, head of the investigation division at Shanghai’s Administration for Market Regulation, was quoted by the jfdaily.com news website as saying the Yangtze River Delta had taken the lead in further clarifying the procedures and boundaries for cross-regional enforcement in market regulation.
He said the move would reduce disruptions to business operations and foster a fair and orderly market environment.
The roll-out comes as Beijing increasingly counts on private firms – which contribute over 60 per cent of national gross domestic product, 70 per cent of technological innovation, and 80 per cent of urban employment – to achieve the country’s annual economic growth target of “around 5 per cent”.
However, private investment remains weak, according to data from the National Bureau of Statistics. In the first 10 months of this year, fixed-asset investment in the private sector shrank 4.5 per cent year on year, while state-sector investment rose 0.1 per cent.

data.title: China web shops use big hang tags, combo locks on clothing to deter exploitative returns
data.description:
E-commerce shops in China are attaching oversized labels on clothes to prevent customers from wearing them before returning under shopping platforms’ return policy.
During China’s Double 11 shopping festival, which falls on November 11 every year, the move by many online shops to attach giant labels and combination locks went viral.
Several shops are attaching giant paper labels, some bigger than A4 paper size, to the clothes they sell, carrying words such as “no return or exchange if the label is removed”.

An online shop based in Beijing also posted on social media that they attached combination locks on the zips of the coats they sell. Only after buyers confirm their purchase would the shop give the code.
The shop owners said this is to curb so-called shearing the sheep behaviour of some customers; the Chinese expression means “taking advantage of shops”.
They believed the lock would make the “sheep shearers” ashamed of wearing the clothes for free.

China’s e-commerce platforms began applying the seven-day unconditional return policy after a revised version of the country’s Consumer Protection Law took effect in 2014.
The policy allows shoppers to return goods bought online without a reason within seven days of receiving the items.
While many returned the clothes they buy after simply trying them out and finding them unsuitable, some used the policy to turn online shops into their free wardrobes.
In recent years, several cases of people returning clothes they had worn made headlines.

In 2024, an e-commerce shop owner complained about students returning seven costumes after having obviously worn them.
The price tags were removed and the items got dirty.
The platform supported the students’ request and gave them a full refund. The shop owner, feeling “hurt”, then exposed the case online.
In 2025, a designer brand exposed that China’s two-man band, Wutiaoren, returned two jackets worth 6,000 yuan (US$840) in total after performing energetically wearing them.
The band’s stylist later apologised and offered to compensate the brand.
A shop owner told the Yangtse Evening Post that e-commerce platforms tend to support buyers. Sellers can appeal the decision but it takes an unnecessary amount of time and energy and does not guarantee satisfactory results.

According to Chinese media company 36Kr, the return rate for women’s apparel online shops is 50 to 60 per cent.
Another owner of a women’s apparel online shop told China Business Herald that returning after wearing is even more common in fair-price formal dresses.
Lawyer Wang Weiwei at Beijing Zhongwen Law Firm told Yangtse Evening Post that customers are obliged to make sure the returned items are in perfect condition; otherwise, the shops have the right to reject refund requests.
“I support the oversized label strategy. I have seen many people travelling while wearing new clothes with the price tags still on them. I despise this behaviour as a consumer because I am afraid of buying returned clothes,” one online observer said.

data.title: South Korean man admits killing girlfriend over insults, storing body in fridge for months
data.description:
A man in South Korea has confessed in court to killing his girlfriend for belittling him, storing her body in a kimchi fridge and impersonating her in text messages to family members for nearly a year.
The defendant, in his 40s and who was not named by local media, strangled his girlfriend of four years on October 20 last year at a home in Gunsan, North Jeolla province, after she berated him for not following her advice on stock investment, according to prosecutors.
He then continued to exchange messages with the victim’s family on her phone and took out a bank loan of 88 million won (US$60,562) under her name, using the money to pay the rent for a villa owned by the woman.
The victim’s younger sister became suspicious when she noticed how her sibling only communicated through text messages. She made a police report in September.
The Chosun daily reported in September that after a first call by police which went unanswered, the sister received a message saying “I am safe and well”, believed to be sent by the defendant through the victim’s phone.
Police later got through on another call, with a woman on the other side of the line. The woman, who was then living with the defendant, admitted to officers she was not the victim and had been asked by him to take the call.
Local news outlet Korea JoongAng Daily cited police who said the body was found mostly intact, as the man had set the kimchi fridge’s temperature to minus 32 degrees Celsius (minus 25.6 Fahrenheit).
The defendant pleaded guilty to murder, abandonment or concealment of a body and fraud involving computer use at the case’s first trial on Thursday at a District Court in Jeonju.
He told police during questioning that he had killed his girlfriend in a fit of rage after feeling belittled by her.
The defence lawyer on Thursday sought a delay in proceedings for time to negotiate a settlement with the grieving family.
The court noted the request but also pointed out that the victim’s family had in a letter submission demanded a severe sentence, with no intent to settle.
The next hearing is set for December 11.

data.title: ONE Championship: Superbon vows to defend his crown as Masaaki Noiri eyes another shock
data.description:
Tokyo’s Ariake Arena will host a striking masterclass on Sunday when Thailand’s Superbon Singha Mawynn defends his ONE featherweight kickboxing world title against Japan’s Masaaki Noiri, the interim champion who stunned Tawanchai PK Saenchai earlier this year.
The bout, which puts national pride on the line for both fighters, headlines ONE 173, a card stacked with elite strikers.
Both men understand what is at stake: Superbon wants to cement his legacy as an all-time great, while Noiri aims to deliver another defining moment for Japanese kickboxing.
Whatever happens in Tokyo, ONE 173’s main event promises to showcase the very best of the featherweight division.
Speaking to the Post in Tokyo, Superbon said he felt rejuvenated as the division filled up with world-class contenders.


“It’s an exciting time because all the fighters are good and coming up,” he said. “I’ve been here a long time, but I’ll still be here to beat them up.”
He admitted he still kept an eye on long-time rival Marat Grigorian, who will meet Rukiya Anpo on the same card.
“Marat is still my top opponent,” Superbon said. “From my generation, only the two of us are left at this level. I always watch him. If he wins, maybe we fight again.”
Superbon said he drew inspiration from his idol, Buakaw Banchamek, whose K-1 triumphs made Thai kickboxing a global spectacle.
“I love the Japanese fans. When Buakaw won, they still supported him. He’s my idol and my teacher. I’ll be here to show what I learned from him.”

On Noiri’s famed left kick, the champion smiled.
“For me, it’s not good for him. He’s not strong on the left. I don’t worry about that.
“I’m the champion. I didn’t come to lose. I came to win and show that I’m still one of the best strikers on the planet.”
Noiri, meanwhile, carries the confidence of a man who has already overturned the odds once. His upset of Tawanchai remained one of the biggest surprises of the year, and he insisted lightning would strike twice.
“I’ll knock him out, 100 per cent,” he said. “This will be the biggest fight of my career. It will be a KO.”
The 32-year-old prides himself on versatility rather than any single weapon.
“I’ve prepared for everything, not just the left kick. I can finish with many weapons, so please watch and see what happens.”


Asked about Tawanchai and Anpo both seeking revenge, Noiri said he welcomed the challenge.
“It makes me happy they want to fight again. But if they want that chance, they have to get results first. I’ll be waiting as world champion.”
Fighting at home added extra motivation, he said.
“The fans give me so much energy. From the face-off to the open workout, I can feel their support. It makes me proud to represent Japan.”
And on the expected finish, Noiri agreed with his opponent.
“That’s what ONE Championship means. A fight must end in a knockout. He’ll come to finish me, I’ll come to finish him. It won’t go the distance.”

data.title: Race for first private space station heats up as Nasa plans 2030 ISS retirement
data.description:
With Nasa’s International Space Station set to come out of service in 2030, American aerospace firm Vast has stepped into a frenzied race for the world’s first commercial space station.
Haven-1 - a mini station scheduled for launch in May 2026 - has been designed for comfort, according to Andrew Feustel, a former Nasa astronaut now an adviser at Vast.
“It has a three-year lifespan, and over that period of time, we plan to visit the spacecraft with multiple crews of four, four at a time,” he said on the sidelines of the Web Summit in Lisbon.
The California-based firm, founded in 2021 by billionaire Jed McCaleb, aspires to replace the International Space Station with Haven-2, a larger version of the first model.

But Vast faces fierce competition from other contenders, including Axiom Space, Voyager Space in partnership with Airbus, and Amazon founder Jeff Bezos’ Blue Origin.
Hopes rest on securing funding from a Nasa budget of up to US$1.5 billion for the development of commercial space stations, which is set to be awarded in April 2026.
“Space agencies no longer want to manage the infrastructure” of the ISS, said Ugo Bonnet, director of the Spaceflight Institute, which offers training for commercial human missions.
Locked in competition with China, Nasa wants to focus more on crewed mission projects to the moon by the end of the decade and eventually build a base on the lunar surface.
In replacing the ISS, Nasa planned to purchase services rather than manage programmes itself - a real boon for private companies in the space market.
“There are a lot of players that are coming with very aggressive timelines, and we cannot do things in the same way we did in the past”, said Roberto Angelini, director of the Exploration and Science Domain of Thales Alenia Space.

The French-Italian joint venture was set to deliver the first two pressurised modules for Axiom’s planned commercial space station, which could be operational as early as 2028.
It has also manufactured half the pressurised modules for the ISS. The company’s main challenge, however, is to “remain competitive in terms of prices”, according to Angelini.
Nasa spends up to US$4 billion a year on the ISS, roughly a third of the US agency’s annual human space flight budget.
SpaceX’s reusable launch vehicles have revolutionised the sector, lowering transportation costs and paving the way for these private projects.
Vast planned to send Haven-1 into space on a SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket, while Axiom’s private mission would see astronauts aboard one of their Crew Dragon capsules.
“Just 15 to 20 years ago, sending a kilogram into space cost US$60,000,” Bonnet said. “When Starship, SpaceX’s launcher becomes operational in 2030, the cost will be less than US$200 per kilogram,” he added.

But operating a commercial space station would still cost a hefty sum.
“I’m not sure about their long-term profitability,” said Beatrice Hainaut, a space policy researcher at the Institute for Strategic Research at the Military School.
Companies were counting on increased demand from governments and the private sector to generate revenue.
Vast has predicted that 85 per cent of its crewed mission revenues would come from state agencies, and 15 per cent from private clients.
Feustel said the company wanted to be a “service provider to not only the US government”, but all countries seeking to send their astronauts into space for training and research.
“For less than US$100 million, you can put an astronaut in space on a Vast spacecraft.”

data.title: Hong Kong medical student gets 1 year probation for filming men in dorm showers
data.description:
A medical student from Hong Kong’s top university has been sentenced to a year’s probation after admitting to secretly filming two hallmates while they were showering in a dormitory bathroom.
Chung Chun-yu, 23, earlier pleaded guilty to two counts of voyeurism and told Principal Magistrate David Cheung Chi-wai that his act of using a mobile phone to film other students through a hole in a laundry basket at a University of Hong Kong hall in September last year was a one-off incident.
The defendant said he did not suffer from voyeuristic disorder but admitted to police that he was attracted to the male body and had made the recordings to satisfy his sexual desires.
Eastern Court heard on Friday that a probation officer’s report found Chung had shown genuine remorse. Magistrate Cheung also noted that the student had taken steps to address his issues by undergoing therapy.
“Value this second chance,” Cheung told the defendant as he imposed the probation sentence.
On the night of September 22, 2024, a 23-year-old male resident of the Madam S H Ho Residence for Medical Students discovered a mobile phone covered by a towel in a laundry basket that had been placed covertly nearby.
He later found Chung hiding in a cubicle and confronted him at the scene.
The device had recorded a 39-minute video showing the naked bodies of the resident and another male in the shower. The resident subsequently reported the matter to police.
